Social constructionists, by contrastive argumentation and deconstruction of claims made in both schools' literature review section: critical realism has been found out as a more useful tool for researchers who want their findings to have practical implications (Hammersley 1992). On the other hand, social constructionists hold that reality is constructed through language rather than discovered or observed. They argue there are no objective facts but only interpretations and these should be recognized in any scientific investigation of health phenomena. This article aims at providing a comprehensive overview on how positivism as well its two prominent schools postpositivist - critical realism, social constructionism- position themselves regarding knowledge creation process within the contextual field called 'health sciences'. It seeks to highlight their philosophical orientations and compare these approaches with one another. In doing so it aims at contributing a deeper understanding for those who are interested

texts.full_text: about Centura Health's journey with different EHR systems, likely referring to their switch from Epic to Meditech and then back to Epic: "Re: Epic. See the first comment after this article. I was subject to this during my time at Epic. The decision-making process around switching EMR/EHR platforms is incredibly and often involves than just cost considerations; it can be a mix of workflow, user satisfaction, support quality, integration capabilities with other systems, and long-term strategic goals for the healthcare organization. From an insider's perspective, seeing organizations like Centura Health go from Epic to Meditech and then back again highlights how critical ongoing evaluation is. The move might have been driven by immediate cost savings or a perceived fit at that time, but as Carol R points out, there are often deeper lessons learned along the way. For instance, while Meditech may offer solid functionality and can be more affordable in some respects, organizations sometimes find themselves missing Epic's advanced features, user-friendly interface, robust support network, and data analytics capabilities. The return to Epic after a trial with another system suggests that initial assessments might not have fully captured all the long-term benefits or challenges of each platfor

texts.full_text: paper, we address the most relevant form of perturbation that may affect a graph: percolation. In a percolation graph links between nodes are created with a certain probability $p$. A generalization of the static percolation, where the links can be created and destroyed in time with a certain rate, is called dynamical percolation [@druger83]. If the percolation rate vanishes, the static case is recovered. For CTQW, the absence or presence of a link between two nodes of the underlying graph is identified with the corresponding tunneling amplitude between the walker sites, which may take a zero or non-zero value, i.e. it can flip between two values. this duality allows us to further generalize the percolation model by assuming that the coupling between sites can randomly switch between any two non-zero values, thus mimicking the fact that the weights of the edges are dichotomic random variables. We call this dynamical noise *generalized percolation*, since it includes dynamical percolation as a special case. texts.full_text: or changes that occur in the region during the fetal period. While investigations in our laboratory have begun to chart these changes through the use of postmortem materials, in vivo studies have been rarely attempted. This study combines ultrasonography with new applications of video editing to examine aspects of prenatal respiratory development. Structures of the fetal upper respiratory-digestive tract and their movements were studied through the use of ultrasonography and detailed frame-by-frame analysis. Twenty-five living fetuses, aged 18-36 wweeks gestation (GA), were examined using a GE Logic Echomaster ultrasound machine with a 7 MHz transducer to visualize movement in this region. In each case, the upper respiratory tract was scanned for an average of five minutes. A total of three hundred and fifty-five images were taken during these sessions. The image quality obtained allowed us to identify various structures such as pharynx (nasopharyngeal area), epiglottis, laryngotracheal region with vocal cords attached at the anterior wall. texts.full_text: . - Operation time was longer in the laparoscopic group (median 180 vs. 150 minutes), but this was offset by intraoperative blood loss and fewer wound-related complications. 2. Postoperative Complications: - Short-term complications (e.g., anastomotic leak, intra-abdominal abscess) occurred at similar rates between groups (12% vs. 14%). - Laparoscopic surgery was associated with a lower incidence of site infections (5% vs. 18%) and reduced risk of hernias. 3. Recurrence of Crohn’s Disease: - Long-term recurrence rates (assessed via clinical, endoscopic, and imaging criteria over 5 years) were between laparoscopic and open approaches (38% vs. 41% at 5 years). - No significant differences were observed in the frequency or severity of recurrence, including strictures or fistulas, suggesting surgical approach does not influence disease behavior. 4. Reoperation Rates: - Both groups had similar reoperation rates for recurrent disease (22% vs. 25%), with no evidence that laparoscopic surgery increased the likelihood of reoperation. Implications: - Laparoscopic ileo-colonic resection is a safe and effective alternative to open surgery for Crohn’s disease, offering equivalent long-term recurrence outcomes while improving short-term recovery and reducing complications. - Surgeons should prioritize patient selection and technical expertise to optimize laparoscopic outcomes, as recurrence risk remains primarily driven by disease-specific factors (e.g., preoperative inflammation, location

texts.full_text: 7 F.2d 61 John M. RAY, Plaintiff-Appellant,v.Earl HUDDLESTON, John Sloan and Frank Summers, Defendants-Appellees. No. 15368. United States Court of Appeals Sixth Circuit. Jan. 14, 1964. Douglas E. Robertson, Bowling Green, Ky. (Duncan & Allender, Bowling Green, Ky., on brief), for appellant. Paul R. Huddleston, Bowling Green, Ky. (Huddleston & Huddleston, Bowling Green, Ky., on brief), for appellees. Before O'SULLIVAN, Circuit Judge, and McALLISTER and MAGRUDER, Senior Circuit Judges. PER CURIAM. 1 Appellant, John M. Ray, sued appellee Earl Huddleston, claiming that the latter, a county judge of the county of Clinton, Kentucky, had illegally exercised his power by ordering the construction of a road which would have benefited the appellees John Sloan and Frank Summers, who were also named defendants, but not the general public. Appellant alleged that this action constituted an unlawful expenditure of public funds and an ultra vires act. 2 The District Court granted a motion to dismiss the complaint for failure to state a claim upon which relief could be granted. The court held that under the law of Kentucky, a taxpayer in the position of appellant could not maintain an action of this kind. The complaint did not allege any private injury to the appellant, as distinguished from the general harm alleged to have been suffered by all the taxpayers of the county. 3 Appellant urges that this case comes within an exception recognized in some jurisdictions, where a public official is accused of acting without any legal basis and in such a way as to result in the waste of public funds, thus allowing a suit by a taxpayer. However, the cases cited by appellant in support of this position do not control in Kentucky. The applicable Kentucky decisions have consistently required some special injury to a plaintiff in order to permit an action of this type. 4 We find no error in the decision of the District Court. The judgment is affirmed.

texts.full_text: an additional means of propulsion if these animals were partially aquatic or needed to navigate through dense vegetation on land. Skull Characteristics the skull structure of Araeoscelidians, particularly evident in well-preserved specimens like , showcases several distinctive features: - Temporal Fenestration: The presence of two temporal openings (fenestrae) is a defining characteristic. These fenestrae reduced the weight of the skull and provided attachment points for powerful jaw muscles. - Teeth: Their teeth were typically small, sharp, and conical, suitable for catching insects or other small prey items typical in their environment. Ecology Araeoscelidians likely occupied a variety of ecological niches: - Diet: They are thought to have been insectivorous or omnivorous, feeding on the diverse array of arthropods and possibly smaller vertebrates available during the Late Carboniferous and Early Permian. - Habitat: While primarily terrestrial, some genera like show adaptations that suggest a semi-aquatic lifestyle. This diversity in habitat preference would have allowed them to exploit different resources within their environments. Evolutionary Significance As the sister group to all later diapsids, Araeoscelidians are crucial for understanding early reptilian evolution: - Phylogenetic Position: Their position on the evolutionary tree provides insights into the transition from more primitive sauropsids (like captorhinomorphs) to a wide array of later groups including archosaurs and lepidosaurs. - Anatomical Innovations: The development of diapsid skull structure in Araeoscelidians represents an important anatomical innovation that would be further refined in descendant lineages. These methods can produce parts with extremely fine details, smooth surfaces, and even complex internal structures that traditional 3D printers struggle to achieve. ### The Impact on Industries 1. Potential problems could be: * Misspelled the module name * Need to add a source directory or new dependency to elm-package.json The List.Extra modHere's the completed text: The List.Extra module was not installed as a separate package, but rather is part of another package. To fix this issue, you need to import it from its parent package instead. For example, if 'List.Extra' is part of the `elm-list-extra` package, your corrected code would be: ```haskell import List.Extra exposing (..) ``` However, in your case, since Elm's List.Extra package was installed separately and you have it listed as a dependency in your elm-package.json file, try adding the following lines to your elm-package.json: ```json "elm-list-extra": { "version": "^0.1.5" } ``` And then run `elm update` or `npm install` (or whatever package manager you're using) to ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date. texts.full_text: of Shigella flexneri Specific Oligosaccharides Using Disaccharides as Potential Transglucosylase Acceptor Substrates. Chemo-enzymatic strategies hold great potential for the development of stereo- and regioselective syntheses of structurally defined bioactive oligosaccharides. Herein, we illustrate the potential of the appropriate combination of a planned chemo-enzymatic pathway and an engineered biocatalyst for the multistep synthesis of an important decasaccharide for vaccine development. We report the stepwise investigation, which led to an efficient chemical conversion of allyl α-d-glucopyranosyl-(1→4)-α-l-rhamnopyranosyl-(1→3)-2-deoxy-2-trichloroacetamido-β-d-glucopyranoside, the product of site-specific enzymatic α-d-glucosylation of a lightly protected non-natural disaccharide acceptor, into a pentasaccharide building block suitable for chain elongation at both ends. texts.full_text: and basketballAthletes had one of the highest body mass indices (BMI; p < 0.05) among other sports participants in this study group, indicating a variety within athletic populations that standard RMR prediction equations often do not account for adequately due to significant variation based on sport type and specific physiological demands they impose upon an athlete's physique such as lean body mass or fat percentage distribution which directly influence metabolic rate. texts.full_text: Fitter From Left to Right: Paul Swift, founder of Bikefit Systems; Antony Pringle of The Bike Energy Lab; Eric Atnip, Lousiville KY-based fitter, coach, and race organizer at Mideast Multisport; and Dan Empfield, developer of the F.I.S.T. fitting methodology and founder of Slowtwitch.com Antony started fitting people to bikes at Cycles Bertrand, a custom frame builder in Gatineau, Quebec, in the mid-eighties, an era in which the shop was deemed Bicycling Magazine’s best bike shop in North America, and one of the first shops of the world to adopt tHe Fit Kit, the world’s first tool for bike fitting. He developed a comprehensive understanding of bicycle frame geometry and design principles, and a knowledge of component and equipment selection His passion for bicycles and speed led him to pursue an education in engineering. He holds a degree in engineering from the University of Waterloo, a Master's degree in mechanical engineering from the University of Ottawa, and has taken advanced courses in ergonomics (the fitting of humans to machines), aerodynamical drag measurements using wind tunnel tests. He worked as bike-fitter for Trek's pro racing teams. He then went on staff with LeMond Bicycles where he helped develop aero frames used by the US Postal Team and designed saddles still being sold today After two years of working in industry, Antony decided to focus his expertise exclusively upon fitting people up onto bikes. Since founding The Bike Energy Lab Ltd., has worked as bike-fitter for Trek's pro racing teams. But although it’s a journey that can certainly involve schools there are many other organisations that need to do more work – and work in a more joined-up manner – to make a real impact on our streets. One of the other stakeholders involved in the dRive to reduce knife crime is, of course, the police – they are doing such a good job to use their limited resources to try and make a difference. And they are working with schools every day – not just dealing with crimes and teenagers taking knives into school but also by delivering effective preventative measures. Last academic year, I took my form group to join the rest of their year in the school hall to watch an innovative presentation from South Yorkshire Police officers. Guns and Knives Cost Lives had been used by the police force before and was relaunched in 2017 to help deal with the increasing problem of knife crime among young people. In the first year since the rebooted and hard-hitting presentation started to travel around schools, 143 sessions were delivered to a student audience of over 20,000. The talk was pitched at exactly the right level; there was intentionally nothing fun or enjoyable about sitting through the Guns and Knives Cost Lives talk, there was no real attempt to make this an overly entertaining session. And neither should there have been. Police running the event intended to pull the horrific curtain on the shocking reality of crime using guns and knives – this was a session of shocking the students to sense. Fearsome anecdotes were followed by tear-jerking anecdotes as we learnt about the true consequences of knife crime through a series of real-life tales that only the police would have to hand. Not recommended due to unpredictable traffic which may cause you to miss the tour. Tour Bus Loading/ Unloading and Parking: Click here (opens in a new window) for DOT's permitted tour bus map in lower Manhattan. Tour ends at the 9/11 Memorial (about 4 blocks from the meeting location) ST. PAUL’S CHAPEL We will start the tour at St. Paul's Chapel, New York City’s oldest public building still in continuous use. Just one block away from the 9/11 attacks, it miraculously survived the falling building and debris. St. Paul’s Chapel served as a makeshift rescue center during the aftermath. Its iron fence became a bulletin board where family members posted notices of their missing loved ones. Today, with the mementos received from people around the world supporting New York City, the church serves as both a landmark and a tribute. 9/11 MEMORIAL You'll enter the 9/11 Memorial, where you can pay your respects to those who lost their lives in the World Trade Center. Take a moment at Michael Arad’s “Reflecting Absence,” a man-made waterfall that symbolizes the void left behind by tragedy. Learn how the names of the nearly 3,000 victims were placed around the waterfall next to their friends, family members, and colleagues. See the new Freedom Tower and World Trade Center rise from the ground. 9/11 MUSEUM You will be provided with timed entry tickets to minimize wait times. Museum entry time will be based on availability. Our guides will give you a preview of the museum and what to expect so you can plan accordingly, and still have time to see other sights important to you. Morane-Saulnier's MS.325 design was selected by the Service Technique de l' Aeronautique on 10 November 1931, but the design was never produced in large numbers. The MS.325 was a low-wing monoplane with an all-metal fuselage. The aircraft had retractable landing gear and was powered by a liquid-cooled engine. The MS.325 was not selected by the French military and was abandoned in 1934. texts.full_text: k fort that lies within the park. The fort was one of several designed to defend Washington from a Confederate attack during the Civil War. There are few remains of the actual fortifications. It is one of Washington's largest parks and protects an important sub-watershed of the Anacostia River. The park is a popular place for picnics, nature walks, indoor ice skating, mountain bike riding, gardening, environmental education, music, skating, sports, and ranger-led Civil War programs. Adjacent parks Fort Dupont Park is adjacent to a series of other national and DC parks. Commonly people refer to all of them together as Fort Dupont. These are the adjacent parks: US government: Fort Chaplin Park Fort Davis Park Fort Stanton Park Smithsonian Anacostia Community Museum DC government: Benning Stoddert Recreation Center Alger Park Fort Battery Ricketts Avalon Playground Facilities Concert venue Fort Dupont has a 40-year history of offering a summer concert series. Many famous musicians and bands in the Washingtonarea have performed at the park, including local jazz and blues musicians. The concert series typically runs from June to August and features a variety of music genres, including jazz, blues, gospel, and R&B. The concerts are free and open to the public, and attendees are encouraged to bring blankets, chairs, and picnics to enjoy during the performances. The concert venue is located in a natural amphitheater, providing a unique and intimate setting for music lovers to enjoy live music under the stars. texts.full_text: aining structural strength. cables can often be routed through these voids. The hardest part might be finding a void. The blocks are staggered in a wall, but the voids still usually line up. You probably need to cut a hole in the drywall just above the top of the block wall. You may be able to see the voids, or you may need to drill test holes through the lower framing plate until you find one. What happens next depends on where you are routing the wires and what is below the cinder block wall. You can drill through the cinder block at the base of the wall directly below the void and route the wires to either side of the wall. However, if you are routing cables down from a fully framed upper section into an area that continues beneath as part of your basement space (not necessarily on another level), there might be alternative options for routing: 1) Use Cable Management Boxes: You can install cable management boxes or raceways directly above the cinder block wall. texts.full_text: as we have seen, starts from a double decree of absolute predestination, which antedates creation, and is the divine program of history. This program includes the successive stages of the creation of man, an universal fall and condemnation of the race, a partial redemption and salvation, and a partial reprobation and perdition: all for the glory of god and the display of his attributes of mercy and justice. History is only the execution of this eternal plan, and every event, including the fall of man and the crucifixion of Christ, is a link in the chain of an unalterable necessity. This system is based on the sovereignty of God, and the idea that he is the sole and absolute cause of all that is, and that his will is the ultimate reason of every event. It is a vast and consistent, but and terrifying, machine, which crushes all human freedom, and reduces man to a mere automaton. texts.full_text: The skyway network links office towers, hotels, banks, corporate and governmeNt buildings, restaurants, and retail stores across downtown. Key connections include Nicollet Mall, Mayo Clinic Square, Target Center, Target Field, and U.S. Bank Stadium. In scale, it rivals tunnel systems in Houston, Toronto, Montreal, Calgary, and Edmonton, making it a vital urban infrastructure that supports a vibrant, walkable city core. Several condominium and apartment complexes are skyway-connected as well, allowing residents to live, work, and shop downtown without having to leave the skyway sysTem.
